slides.title: The Alarm on the Fake File
slides.subtitle: Detecting Attacks on Data: how defenders notice an adversary who already got through — accounting logs, honeypots, and cryptographic hashes — and how to choose, evaluate, and apply them
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Unit 5, Securing Applications and Data, and the topic that closes it — Lesson 5.6, Detecting Attacks on Data. Every topic before this one was about prevention: hardening code, sanitizing inputs, keeping adversaries out. Today we assume the adversary may get in anyway and ask a different question — how do we notice? You'll meet the three detective controls the College Board names: accounting and log analysis, the honeypot, and the cryptographic hash. Then you'll learn to choose among them by cost and sensitivity, weigh what each one can and cannot catch, verify a file's integrity by comparing hashes, and finally read real log lines for the fingerprints of an attack. This single deck covers all five learning objectives, 5.6.A through E.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how attacks on data are detected using accounting/log analysis, honeypots, and cryptographic hashes (LO 5.6.A)
Determine which detective control fits a situation using cost, sensitivity/criticality, and data classification (LO 5.6.B)
Evaluate a detection method's impact — its detection speed and its false-negative blind spots (LO 5.6.C)
Identify whether a file has been altered by recording and comparing its hash value (LO 5.6.D)
Apply detection techniques to spot and report SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflow, and directory traversal in log files (LO 5.6.E)

slides.script: Five objectives today, one for each learning objective in the topic. First, 5.6.A — explain how detection actually works using the three controls the CED names. Second, 5.6.B — determine which control to use, weighing cost, the sensitivity of the data, and its legal classification. Third, 5.6.C — evaluate impact, meaning how fast a method detects and, just as important, what it misses — its false negatives. Fourth, 5.6.D — the hands-on skill of verifying a file by hashing it and comparing digests. Fifth, 5.6.E — reading log files for the signatures of four named attacks. The thread running through all of it: detection is not prevention.

slides.prompt: An administrator drops a file named 'executive_payroll_passwords.xlsx' onto a shared server. It contains only fake data — no real passwords. The system is set to text the security team the instant anyone opens that file.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: why would a defender plant a file that holds nothing real? What does an alert from this file tell the team that a normal access log might not? Who is the only kind of person who would ever open it?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own, no notes. An admin plants a file called 'executive_payroll_passwords.xlsx' — sounds like a jackpot — but it's full of fake data, and the system alerts security the second anyone opens it. In three to five sentences: why plant a file with nothing real inside? What does opening it tell you that an ordinary access log might not? And who is the only kind of person who would ever go looking for it? Hold your answers. By the end of today you'll know this control by name — a honeypot — and exactly why any access to it is an indicator of malicious activity.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have five sections, one per learning objective. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: How Detection Works (5.6.A)
sections.description: The three detective controls the CED names — accounting/log analysis, honeypots, and cryptographic hashes — and what each one reveals (EK 5.6.A.1–A.3).

sections.title: Choosing a Control (5.6.B)
sections.description: Three criteria for picking a detective control: cost, sensitivity/criticality, and data classification (EK 5.6.B.1–B.3).

sections.title: Evaluating Impact (5.6.C)
sections.description: Detection speed — real-time vs. retrospective — and the false negatives that hashes and honeypots miss (EK 5.6.C.1–C.3).

sections.title: Verifying a File by Hashing (5.6.D)
sections.description: Why hashes are repeatable, the commands that generate them, and the record-then-compare workflow (EK 5.6.D.1–D.3).

sections.title: Reading Logs for Attacks (5.6.E)
sections.description: The log signatures of SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflow, and directory traversal (EK 5.6.E.1–E.4).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 5.6 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Five sections in your Guided Notes today, matching the five objectives. Section one is how detection works — the three controls and what each reveals. Section two is how to choose a control, using cost, sensitivity, and classification. Section three is evaluating impact — speed and blind spots. Section four is the hands-on hashing workflow. And section five, marked blue as a deeper dive, is reading actual log lines for four attack signatures. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.label: How Attacks on Data Are Detected
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Data · Learning Objective 5.6.A
slides.script: Section one, learning objective 5.6.A. Before we choose or evaluate anything, we need the three core detective controls the College Board names. Accounting and log analysis, the honeypot, and the cryptographic hash. Roughly twelve minutes here.

slides.heading: Accounting: Logging Who Touched What (EK 5.6.A.1)
bullets: Devices track and log when data are accessed and by whom — the process of recording and monitoring user activities is called accounting (EK 5.6.A.1)
Analyzing these logs can reveal malicious activity when an adversary tries to access, copy, move, or delete data
Suspicious signal: accessing files that aren’t typically accessed
Suspicious signal: access outside a user’s normal patterns — unusual time of day, location, or device type
Suspicious signal: attempts to delete or copy sensitive files
slides.script: Start with accounting. Every device tracks and logs when data are accessed and by whom — that recording and monitoring of user activity is what the CED calls accounting. The point isn't the log itself; it's that analyzing the log reveals malicious activity. What are we looking for? Three patterns: someone accessing files they never touch, someone acting outside their normal pattern — wrong time of day, strange location, an unfamiliar device — and attempts to delete or copy sensitive files. Picture an accountant opening engineering files at 3 a.m. from a laptop nobody's seen. That's accounting earning its keep. Write the definition and all three suspicious signals with EK 5.6.A.1.

slides.heading: Two More Controls: The Honeypot and the Hash
slides.subheading: EK 5.6.A.2 and A.3 — one baits the adversary, the other proves a file is unchanged.

items.title: Honeypot
items.summary: A file that appears to hold valuable data — credit card numbers, PII, passwords — but whose contents are fake. The system alerts defenders if anyone tries to access it. Because there is no legitimate reason to open a fake file, any attempted access is an indicator of malicious activity.

items.title: Cryptographic Hash
items.summary: A cryptographic hash function generates a digest for data and can reveal if the data have been altered. If a file changes unexpectedly, that can be a sign of malicious activity — the hash is how you notice the change.

slides.script: Two more controls. EK 5.6.A.2, the honeypot — that's the bell-ringer file. It looks like it holds something valuable, credit card numbers, PII, passwords, but the data inside are fake. The system alerts defenders the instant anyone opens it, and here's the elegant part: because there's no legitimate reason to access a fake file, any access at all is an indicator of malicious activity. No false alarms from normal users. EK 5.6.A.3, the cryptographic hash — a hash function generates a digest, a fingerprint, for data, and can reveal if the data have been altered. A file changing unexpectedly is a sign of trouble, and the hash is how you catch it. Write both with their EK numbers.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 5.6.A)

terms.definition: The process of recording and monitoring user activities — devices log when data are accessed and by whom, and analysis of those logs can reveal malicious activity (EK 5.6.A.1).
A file that appears to contain valuable data but holds fake data; any attempted access signals malicious activity because there is no legitimate reason to open it (EK 5.6.A.2).
A function that generates a digest for data and can reveal if the data have been altered, since an unexpected change can indicate malicious activity (EK 5.6.A.3).
The fixed output a cryptographic hash function produces for a given input; comparing digests over time shows whether a file changed (EK 5.6.A.3).

slides.script: Four terms for section one. Accounting is recording and monitoring user activity so log analysis can surface malicious access. A honeypot is a fake-data file whose every access is a malicious-activity indicator. A cryptographic hash function generates a digest and reveals alteration. And the digest itself is that fixed output — the fingerprint you compare over time. Get all four into your notes with the EK numbers; the exam expects you to name the control from a scenario.

slides.label: Choosing a Detective Control
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Data · Learning Objective 5.6.B
slides.script: Section two, learning objective 5.6.B. You know the controls — now how do you choose? The CED gives three criteria: cost, sensitivity or criticality, and data classification.

slides.heading: Criterion 1 — Cost (EK 5.6.B.1)
bullets: Honeypots and using hash values to check data integrity are inexpensive detective controls (EK 5.6.B.1)
Some organizations invest in third-party data loss prevention (DLP) services for stronger detection — at a higher cost
DLP monitors data access, usage, and transmission by users throughout the organization to detect suspicious activity
Trade-off: cheap controls (honeypots, hashes) cover the basics; DLP buys broader, stronger coverage for more money
slides.script: Criterion one is cost. Good news for a tight budget: honeypots and hash-based integrity checks are inexpensive — you can stand them up with almost no spend. If an organization wants stronger, broader detection, it can buy a third-party data loss prevention service, a DLP, which monitors data access, usage, and transmission across the whole organization. DLP gives strong detection capabilities, but at a higher cost. So the trade-off is simple: cheap controls cover the basics, DLP buys broader coverage for more money. Write the cheap-versus-DLP contrast with EK 5.6.B.1 — the exam loves to invert this and say honeypots are expensive.

slides.heading: Criteria 2 and 3 — Sensitivity and Classification
slides.subheading: EK 5.6.B.2 and B.3 — what the data is worth, and what the law says about watching it.

left.title: Sensitivity / Criticality (EK 5.6.B.2)
left.definition: More valuable data, watched more closely
examples: Sensitivity or criticality of data or applications is a criterion for choosing detective controls
More sensitive or critical data are more likely targets of an adversary
Therefore they should be monitored more closely than low-value systems
Example: a bank watches its core account-balance database far more closely than its public marketing site

right.title: Classification (EK 5.6.B.3)
right.definition: Some data must be watched by law
examples: Classification of data is a criterion for choosing detective controls
Data classified as private, educational, healthcare, or financial often carry legal or regulatory requirements
Those requirements can mandate specific detection and monitoring
Example: a hospital's patient health records carry healthcare-classification monitoring obligations

slides.footer: AP TIP: EK 5.6.B.2 is about VALUE (sensitive/critical data are likelier targets, so watch them more closely) and EK 5.6.B.3 is about LAW (private, educational, healthcare, or financial classifications often REQUIRE monitoring). Don't blur them — value is a judgment call, classification is an obligation.
slides.script: Criteria two and three. On the left, EK 5.6.B.2, sensitivity or criticality: more sensitive or critical data and applications are likelier targets of an adversary, so they should be monitored more closely. A bank watches its account-balance database far harder than its public marketing site — same company, very different value. On the right, EK 5.6.B.3, classification: data classified as private, educational, healthcare, or financial often carry legal or regulatory detection and monitoring requirements. A hospital doesn't just choose to watch patient records closely — healthcare classification can require it. Keep these distinct: 5.6.B.2 is a value judgment, 5.6.B.3 is a legal obligation. Write both with EK numbers.

slides.label: Evaluating the Impact of a Method
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Data · Learning Objective 5.6.C
slides.script: Section three, learning objective 5.6.C. Every control has a cost in speed and a blind spot in coverage. Evaluating impact means knowing how fast a method detects — and what it will quietly miss.

slides.heading: Detection Speed: Real-Time vs. Retrospective
slides.subheading: EK 5.6.C.1 and C.2 — when does the alert arrive, before or after the harm is done?

items.title: Speed Needs Automation
items.summary: To operate at an effective speed, log analysis needs to be augmented with some automation — reading every line by hand is too slow. Honeypots, by contrast, offer near-instantaneous detection.

items.title: Real-Time (alerts during the attack)
items.summary: Some DLP tools, honeypots, and real-time automated log analysis provide alerts as an attack is happening, allowing a prompt response that can stop the attack before it does more harm.

items.title: Retrospective (detects after the fact)
items.summary: Retrospective log analysis and using cryptographic hashes to verify data integrity identify attacks after they have already occurred — useful for investigation, but too late to stop that attack.

slides.script: Start with speed. EK 5.6.C.1: for log analysis to run at an effective speed it has to be augmented with automation — a human reading every line can't keep up — while honeypots give near-instant detection on their own. EK 5.6.C.2 sorts methods into two camps. Real-time methods — some DLP, honeypots, and real-time automated log analysis — alert as the attack is happening, so you can respond and stop more harm. Retrospective methods — after-the-fact log analysis and hash-based integrity checks — only tell you an attack already happened. Both are valuable, but only real-time can stop an attack in progress. Write the two camps and which controls fall in each, with EK 5.6.C.2.

slides.heading: A Matching Hash Doesn’t Mean the Data Is Safe
slides.misconception: If a file’s hash hasn’t changed, then no attacker has touched the data and nothing was stolen.
slides.correction: EK 5.6.C.3 names this exact false negative: cryptographic hash functions only detect whether data have been ALTERED. An adversary can view and steal data — copy it, read it, exfiltrate it — without changing the original, and the hash will not move. Honeypots have their own blind spot: they cannot detect adversaries that never attempt to access them. Knowing a control's blind spot tells you what to pair it with.
slides.script: Here's the most tested idea in 5.6.C — false negatives, the attacks a control quietly misses. The myth: if the hash didn't change, nobody touched the data. Wrong. EK 5.6.C.3 says hashes only detect alteration. An adversary can read and steal a file — copy it to a USB and walk out — without altering the original, and the hash never moves. The honeypot has its own blind spot: it can't catch an adversary who never takes the bait. The lesson isn't that these controls are useless; it's that knowing the blind spot tells you what to pair them with. Write the hash false negative and the honeypot false negative with EK 5.6.C.3.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 5.6.A–C. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A small clinic on a tight budget needs to detect tampering with patient records and catch intruders poking at bait files. Name two inexpensive detective controls it should use for the cost claim. Then name one criterion beyond cost that applies to patient records.

An adversary copies a folder of confidential designs to a USB drive but changes nothing in the originals. The team relied only on hash checks. Explain what happened, name the term for it. Then state one real-time control that might have caught the copy.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: a tight-budget clinic needs to detect tampering and catch intruders at bait files — name two inexpensive controls and cite EK 5.6.B.1 for the cost claim, then name a criterion beyond cost that applies to patient records and cite its EK, which is classification, 5.6.B.3. Question two: an adversary copies confidential designs to USB but changes nothing, and the team used only hash checks. Explain what happened — that's a false negative, EK 5.6.C.3 — and name one real-time control, like a DLP or honeypot, that might have caught it. Go.

slides.label: Verifying a File by Its Hash
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Data · Learning Objective 5.6.D
slides.script: Section four, learning objective 5.6.D — the hands-on skill. You'll learn why hashing works for integrity, the commands that generate a hash on each platform, and the record-then-compare workflow that proves a file was altered.

slides.heading: Repeatability and the Commands (EK 5.6.D.1–D.2)
bullets: Hash functions identify changes because they are repeatable: the same input always produces the same output for a given hash function (EK 5.6.D.1)
So if two hashes of the 'same' file differ, the file itself must have changed
Hashes can be generated at the command line, on a website, or with specialized software (EK 5.6.D.2)
zsh (common on Apple computers): shasum -a 256 testfile
slides.script: Two EKs here. First, why hashing works for integrity, EK 5.6.D.1: hash functions are repeatable — the same input always produces the same output for a given function. That's the whole trick. If you hash a file today and hash the 'same' file next week and the outputs differ, the file changed; repeatability rules out the function being random. Second, EK 5.6.D.2, how you actually generate one — at the command line, a website, or special software. Memorize the three commands, because the exam asks which goes with which platform: Windows PowerShell uses Get-FileHash with -Algorithm SHA256; BASH uses sha256sum; and zsh, common on Apple machines, uses shasum -a 256. Write all three exactly.

slides.heading: Record-Then-Compare: Did the File Change? (EK 5.6.D.3)
slides.context: Hash the file, record the digest, hash it again later, and compare. If the two digests differ for the same algorithm, the file was altered between the two hashings (EK 5.6.D.3). Here a defender checks a critical config file across two dates.
rows: This is the trusted reference value — store it safely
Identical to baseline → file is UNCHANGED (integrity intact)
Differs from baseline → file WAS ALTERED between May 1 and Jun 8
Because hashing is repeatable, a different digest means a changed file — investigate it as possible malicious activity
slides.script: Let's walk the record-then-compare workflow from EK 5.6.D.3 as a worked example. Step one, baseline: on May 1 you hash the config file and record the digest — that abbreviated a91f…3d7c is now your trusted reference. Step two, June 1, you re-hash; the digest is identical, so the file is unchanged, integrity intact. Step three, June 8, you re-hash again and get b04e…9af1 — different from the baseline. That means the file was altered sometime between May 1 and June 8. Step four, interpret: because hashing is repeatable, a different digest for the same algorithm can only mean the file changed — so you investigate it as possible malicious activity. Notice the hash tells you THAT it changed and roughly WHEN, but not who or how. Copy this table into your notes.

slides.label: Reading Logs for Attack Indicators
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Data · Learning Objective 5.6.E
slides.script: Section five, learning objective 5.6.E, and a blue enrichment dive. Now we read actual log lines and spot the fingerprints of four named attacks: SQL injection, cross-site scripting, buffer overflow, and directory traversal.

slides.heading: Two Signatures: SQL Injection and XSS
slides.subheading: EK 5.6.E.1 and E.2 — what to grep for in application and server logs of user input.

left.title: SQL Injection (EK 5.6.E.1)
left.definition: SQL control words and symbols in input
examples: A single or double quote character ( ' or " )
A double dash -- (a comment in SQL)
SQL control words in capitals: WHERE, IN, FROM
Example log input: ' OR 1=1 --  has all three tells at once

right.title: Cross-Site Scripting / XSS (EK 5.6.E.2)
examples: Review user input for suspicious tags
An injected script tag in a comment or form field is the giveaway
Example log input: <script>steal()</script> in a username field

slides.footer: AP TIP: SQL injection (EK 5.6.E.1) = quotes, OR 1=1, --, and CAPITAL SQL words. XSS (EK 5.6.E.2) = <script> tags. Match the symbol to the attack — the exam shows you a raw log line and asks which attack it indicates.
slides.script: First two signatures, the ones that live in user input. On the left, SQL injection, EK 5.6.E.1: review logs of user input for SQL control words and symbols — a single or double quote, Boolean conditions like OR 1=1, a double-dash comment, and capitalized SQL words like WHERE, IN, FROM. The classic tell, quote-OR-1=1-dash-dash, packs three of those into one string. On the right, XSS, EK 5.6.E.2: review input for suspicious tags, especially the script tag. An injected <script> in a username or comment field is the giveaway. Match the symbol to the attack — that's exactly how the exam frames it. Write both with EK numbers.

slides.heading: Name That Attack From the Log Line (EK 5.6.E.1–E.4)
slides.context: The exam shows a raw request and asks which attack it indicates. Match the fingerprint to the attack and cite the EK. The last two rows cover buffer overflow (length checks) and directory traversal (../).
rows: Abnormally long URL / cookie / query string / total request
slides.script: Now all four attacks in one lookup table — this is the 5.6.E skill in its purest form. Row one: input is quote, OR 1=1, double-dash — three SQL tells, so SQL injection, EK 5.6.E.1. Row two: a comment field holds a script tag, so cross-site scripting, EK 5.6.E.2. Row three: a cookie that's 9,800 characters long — abnormally long URL, cookie, query string, or total request length signals a buffer overflow, EK 5.6.E.3; for web apps you detect these by checking how much data the user is sending. Row four: a GET request whose path is full of ../ sequences climbing toward etc/passwd — that's directory traversal, EK 5.6.E.4. Match the indicator to the attack and cite the EK. Copy the whole table; this is high-frequency on the exam.

slides.heading: A Honeypot Does Not Catch Every Intruder
slides.misconception: Deploying a honeypot guarantees you’ll detect anyone who breaks into the system.
slides.correction: EK 5.6.C.3 states honeypots cannot detect adversaries that do not attempt to access them. A honeypot only raises an alert when someone takes the bait file (EK 5.6.A.2) — an intruder who heads straight for the real database and never touches the decoy slips by it. That's why honeypots are paired with accounting/log analysis and integrity checks, not used alone.
slides.script: One more blind spot to nail down before we wrap. The myth: drop a honeypot and you'll catch everyone who breaks in. EK 5.6.C.3 says no — a honeypot can't detect an adversary who never tries to access it. It only fires when someone takes the bait, EK 5.6.A.2. An intruder who walks straight to the real database and ignores the decoy slips right past it. That's exactly why you layer a honeypot with accounting logs and integrity checks rather than leaning on it alone. Write this honeypot blind spot with EK 5.6.C.3.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Apply It
slides.subheading: All four questions map to LO 5.6.D–E. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: On a macOS machine using zsh, write the exact command to generate the SHA-256 hash of a file named testfile. Then state what it means if next week's hash of the same file differs from the one you recorded today.
For each log line, name the attack: (a) input = ' OR 1=1 -- (b) GET /files/../../../../etc/passwd (c) a request whose cookie field is 9,800 characters long (d) comment = <script>alert(1)</script>.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences for #1; label and cite for #2. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Last Stop and Think, six minutes, independent. Question one: write the exact zsh command to SHA-256 hash testfile — that's shasum -a 256 testfile, EK 5.6.D.2 — then say what a differing hash next week means, which is that the file was altered, EK 5.6.D.3. Question two: label each log line with its attack and EK — quote-OR-1=1-dash-dash is SQL injection 5.6.E.1, the ../ path is directory traversal 5.6.E.4, the 9,800-character cookie is buffer overflow 5.6.E.3, and the script tag is XSS 5.6.E.2. Be precise and cite. Go.

slides.heading: Detecting Attacks on Data — In One Slide
points: Three detective controls (5.6.A): accounting/log analysis logs who touched what (EK 5.6.A.1), honeypots are fake-data bait whose access signals malice (EK 5.6.A.2), and cryptographic hashes reveal alteration (EK 5.6.A.3)
Choose a control (5.6.B) by cost — honeypots and hashes are cheap, DLP is stronger but costlier (EK 5.6.B.1) — plus sensitivity/criticality (EK 5.6.B.2) and legal classification (EK 5.6.B.3)
Evaluate impact (5.6.C): real-time methods (some DLP, honeypots, real-time log analysis) alert during an attack; retrospective methods (after-the-fact logs, hash checks) detect after (EK 5.6.C.1–C.2)
Mind the false negatives (5.6.C.3): hashes miss data that's viewed/stolen but unaltered; honeypots miss adversaries who never take the bait
Verify a file (5.6.D): hashing is repeatable (EK 5.6.D.1), generate with Get-FileHash / sha256sum / shasum -a 256 (EK 5.6.D.2), then record-then-compare to prove alteration (EK 5.6.D.3)
Read logs (5.6.E): SQL injection = quotes/OR 1=1/-- (E.1), XSS = <script> (E.2), buffer overflow = long fields (E.3), directory traversal = ../ (E.4)
slides.next: Topic 5.6 closes Unit 5 — the big idea to carry to the exam is that detection is not prevention: every method here tells you an attack happened or is happening, and each has a blind spot that tells you what to pair it with.
slides.script: One slide to tie it all together. Three controls under 5.6.A — accounting logs who touched what, honeypots bait the adversary, hashes reveal alteration. Choose among them under 5.6.B by cost, sensitivity, and classification. Evaluate their impact under 5.6.C — real-time versus retrospective, and the false negatives where hashes miss theft-without-change and honeypots miss anyone who skips the bait. Verify a file under 5.6.D with repeatable hashing and the record-then-compare workflow and the three platform commands. And read logs under 5.6.E for the four signatures — quotes and OR 1=1 for SQLi, script tags for XSS, long fields for buffer overflow, and dot-dot-slash for directory traversal. The one idea to carry to the exam: detection is not prevention, and every control here has a blind spot that tells you what to pair it with.

guide.title: Lesson 5.6 — Detecting Attacks on Data — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 5 · Topic 5.6 · 7 class periods
overview: Topic 5.6 closes Unit 5 by shifting from preventing attacks on applications and data to detecting them. Where earlier topics hardened code and inputs, 5.6 assumes an adversary may get through and asks: how do defenders notice? Students learn the detective controls the CED names — log analysis (accounting), honeypots, and cryptographic hashes — then learn to choose among them by cost, sensitivity, and data classification (5.6.B), to weigh their impact and limits including detection speed and false negatives (5.6.C), to verify a file's integrity by comparing hash values (5.6.D), and finally to read log files for the signatures of SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflow, and directory traversal attacks (5.6.E).

Across seven days the lesson moves from concepts (A: what detection is and the three core controls) to evaluation (B and C: how to pick a control and what each one can and cannot catch) to hands-on skill (D: hashing a file and comparing digests; E: spotting attack indicators in real log lines). The recurring exam idea is that detection is not prevention: a hash tells you a file changed but not who read it, and a honeypot only catches an adversary who takes the bait.
los: LO 5.6.A — Explain how to detect attacks on data.
LO 5.6.B — Determine controls for detecting attacks against applications or data.
LO 5.6.C — Evaluate the impact of a method for detecting attacks against an application or data.
LO 5.6.D — Identify whether a file has been altered by verifying its hash.
LO 5.6.E — Apply detection techniques to identify and report indicators of application attacks by analyzing log files.
eks: EK 5.6.A.1 — Devices track and log when data are accessed and by whom. The process of recording and monitoring user activities is called accounting. Analysis of these logs can reveal malicious activity when an adversary attempts to access, copy, move, or delete data. Suspicious activity can include: accessing files that aren't typically accessed; accessing files or applications outside of a user's normal patterns (including time of day, location, and device type); attempts to delete or copy sensitive files.

EK 5.6.A.2 — A honeypot is a file that appears as if it contains valuable data (e.g., credit card information, PII, passwords), but the data in the file are fake. A system can alert defenders if someone attempts to access the honeypot. Since the honeypot is a fake file, there is no legitimate reason to be accessing it, and any attempted access would be an indicator of malicious activity.
EK 5.6.A.3 — Cryptographic hash functions can generate a digest for data and can reveal if data have been altered. If a file has changed unexpectedly, this can be a sign of malicious activity.

EK 5.6.B.1 — Cost is a criterion in determining detective controls. Detective controls like honeypots and using hash values to check data integrity are inexpensive. Some organizations invest in third-party data loss prevention (DLP) services, which monitor data access, usage, and transmission by users throughout the organization to detect suspicious activity; DLP services provide strong detection capabilities at a higher cost.

EK 5.6.B.2 — Sensitivity or criticality of data or applications is a criterion in determining detective controls. More sensitive or critical data or applications are more likely targets of an adversary and should be monitored more closely.

EK 5.6.B.3 — Classification of data is a criterion in determining detective controls. Data that have been classified as private, educational, healthcare, or financial often have legal or regulatory detection and monitoring requirements.
EK 5.6.C.1 — To operate at an effective speed, log analysis needs to be augmented with some automation. Honeypots offer near instantaneous detection capabilities.

EK 5.6.C.2 — Some DLP tools, honeypots, and realtime automated log analysis provide alerts as an attack is happening. These tools allow for a prompt response that can stop an attack before it does more harm. Retrospective log analysis and the use of cryptographic hashes to verify data integrity identify attacks after they have occurred.

EK 5.6.C.3 — False negatives can occur in applications and data attack detection. Cryptographic hash functions only detect if data have been altered. An adversary could view and steal data without altering it, and a cryptographic hash function would not detect this. Honeypots cannot detect adversaries that do not attempt to access them.
EK 5.6.D.1 — Cryptographic hash functions can help identify changes in a file because they are repeatable: the same input always produces the same output for a given hash function.

EK 5.6.D.2 — Hashes can be calculated using the command line on a computer, a website, or specialized software. In Windows PowerShell: Get-FileHash testfile -Algorithm SHA256. In BASH: sha256sum testfile. In zsh (common on Apple computers): shasum -a 256 testfile.

EK 5.6.D.3 — A file can be hashed and its hash output recorded. Then it can be hashed again later, and the second hash output can be compared to the previous hash output for the same file. If a file's hash changes, then the file was altered between when the first and second hashes were generated.

EK 5.6.E.1 — SQL injection attacks can be detected by reviewing application and server logs of user input for SQL control words and symbols such as: a single or double quote character; Boolean conditions like OR 1=1; a double dash (which indicates a comment in SQL): --; SQL control words (always in capital letters) like WHERE, IN, FROM.
EK 5.6.E.2 — XSS attacks can be detected by reviewing user input for suspicious tags, particularly the <script>...</script> tag.

EK 5.6.E.3 — For web applications, buffer overflows can be detected by checking the amount of data the user is sending to the web application in their request. The fields commonly checked are the URL length, cookie length, query string length, and total request length. Long strings in any of these fields can be an indicator of an attempted buffer overflow attack.

EK 5.6.E.4 — Directory traversal attacks can be detected by reviewing application and server logs. HTTP GET requests that include paths with sequences of ../ are indicators of an adversary attempting a directory traversal.

pacing.focus: How to detect attacks on data: accounting/log analysis, honeypots, and hashes (LO 5.6.A)

segments.segment: QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q1) + bell ringer (the alarm on the fake file)
Objectives + accounting and log analysis (EK 5.6.A.1)
Honeypots: the fake bait file (EK 5.6.A.2)
Cryptographic hashes reveal alteration (EK 5.6.A.3)
Vocab + stop and think
Wrap and assign Your Turn
QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q2) + bell ringer + recap of the three controls
Cost as a criterion: cheap honeypots/hashes vs. higher-cost DLP (EK 5.6.B.1)
What DLP monitors: access, usage, transmission (EK 5.6.B.1)
Stop and think + wrap

pacing.focus: Choosing detective controls: sensitivity and classification (LO 5.6.B)

segments.segment: QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q3) + bell ringer (which data gets watched closest?)
Sensitivity/criticality as a criterion (EK 5.6.B.2)
Classification and legal/regulatory requirements (EK 5.6.B.3)
Stop and think + wrap

pacing.focus: Evaluating impact: detection speed and false negatives (LO 5.6.C)

segments.segment: QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q4) + bell ringer (detect now vs. detect later)
Speed: automation, honeypots near-instant, real-time vs. retrospective (EK 5.6.C.1–C.2)
False negatives: what hashes and honeypots miss (EK 5.6.C.3)
Stop and think + wrap

pacing.focus: Verifying a file's integrity by hashing (LO 5.6.D)

segments.segment: QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q5) + bell ringer (did this file change?)
Repeatability of hash functions (EK 5.6.D.1)
Generating hashes: PowerShell, BASH, zsh commands (EK 5.6.D.2)
Record-then-compare workflow (EK 5.6.D.3) + stop and think

pacing.focus: Reading logs for attack indicators: SQLi, XSS, buffer overflow, directory traversal (LO 5.6.E)

segments.segment: QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q6) + bell ringer (spot the bad request)
SQL injection and XSS signatures in logs (EK 5.6.E.1–E.2)
Buffer overflow length checks and directory traversal ../ (EK 5.6.E.3–E.4)
Log-reading practice + wrap
QOTD (U5/L5.6/Q7) + bell ringer + whole-topic recap
Worked review across all five objectives
Quiz debrief + bridge to the exam

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Lesson Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run it as a block-period extension or as the next lesson’s warm-up (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz already ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

segments.segment: Lesson 5.6 quiz

misconceptions.heading: Detection equals prevention
misconceptions.myth: If a control detects an attack, it also stops the attack from succeeding.
misconceptions.reality: 5.6 is about detective controls. Per EK 5.6.C.2, only real-time methods (some DLP, honeypots, real-time automated log analysis) alert as an attack happens so a response can stop further harm; retrospective log analysis and hash checks identify attacks after they have already occurred.

misconceptions.heading: A matching hash means the data is safe
misconceptions.myth: If a file's hash hasn't changed, no attacker has touched the data.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.6.C.3 names this false negative directly: cryptographic hash functions only detect whether data have been altered. An adversary could view and steal data without altering it, and the hash would not change.

misconceptions.heading: Honeypots catch every intruder
misconceptions.myth: Deploying a honeypot guarantees you'll detect anyone who breaks in.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.6.C.3 states honeypots cannot detect adversaries that do not attempt to access them. A honeypot only raises an alert when someone takes the bait file (EK 5.6.A.2).

misconceptions.heading: Detection is always expensive
misconceptions.myth: Catching attackers requires costly enterprise tooling.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.6.B.1 says detective controls like honeypots and using hash values to check data integrity are inexpensive; it is third-party DLP services that provide strong detection at a higher cost.

support: Give students a three-column reference card — Log analysis / Honeypot / Hash — and have them fill in what each control detects and what it misses, anchored to EK 5.6.A.1–A.3 and 5.6.C.3.
Provide a sentence frame for 5.6.E: 'This log line shows a ____ attack (EK 5.6.E.__) because it contains ____ (e.g., OR 1=1, <script>, ../, an unusually long field).'
extension: Have students argue, using EK 5.6.C.2, when retrospective hash-based integrity checking is still worthwhile even though it only detects attacks after they occur.
Ask students to map each of the three CED data-classification triggers in EK 5.6.B.3 to a real-world regulation, then explain why classification raises monitoring requirements.

discussion: EK 5.6.B lists three criteria for choosing detective controls — cost, sensitivity/criticality, and classification. Which would dominate your decision for a small clinic's patient records, and why?
EK 5.6.C.3 describes false negatives for hashes and honeypots. Does knowing a control's blind spot make it useless, or does it tell you what to pair it with?
EK 5.6.A.1 says accounting logs can reveal access outside a user's normal patterns. What are the privacy trade-offs of monitoring time of day, location, and device type for every employee?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.

quiz.title: Lesson 5.6 — Detecting Attacks on Data — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 14 questions, 25 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A security analyst reviews logs and notices an employee in accounting opened several engineering design files at 3 a.m. from an unfamiliar laptop — files that person has never touched before. Per EK 5.6.A.1, what process produced the records that made this detection possible?
options: Hashing, which records a digest of every file the user opens
Sanitization, which records and rejects malformed user input
Accounting, which records and monitors when data are accessed and by whom
Classification, which records the sensitivity label assigned to each file
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.A.1 defines accounting as the process of recording and monitoring user activities — devices track and log when data are accessed and by whom, and analysis of those logs can reveal malicious activity such as accessing files outside a user's normal patterns of time of day, location, and device type. Hashing (A) checks alteration, sanitization is an input control from an earlier topic, and classification (D) labels data but does not record access events.

questions.stem: An administrator places a file named 'executive_payroll_passwords.xlsx' on a server. The file contains only fabricated data, and the system is set to alert the security team the instant anyone opens it. Which detective control from EK 5.6.A.2 is this?
options: A data loss prevention service monitoring transmission
A honeypot — a fake file whose access signals malicious activity
A cryptographic hash recorded to verify the file's integrity
A retrospective log analysis run against the access records
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.A.2 describes a honeypot as a file that appears to hold valuable data but whose contents are fake; since there is no legitimate reason to access it, any attempted access is an indicator of malicious activity and the system can alert defenders. A DLP service (A) monitors real data flows, a hash (C) checks alteration, and retrospective log analysis (D) reviews records after the fact rather than baiting an attacker.

questions.stem: A defender wants a way to tell, at any later time, whether the contents of a critical configuration file have been changed unexpectedly. According to EK 5.6.A.3, which tool is designed to reveal that data have been altered?
options: An access control vestibule placed at the server room door
A honeypot file seeded among the configuration directory
A data classification label applied to the configuration file
A cryptographic hash function that generates a digest for the data
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.A.3 states that cryptographic hash functions can generate a digest for data and can reveal if data have been altered; an unexpected change can be a sign of malicious activity. A honeypot (B) detects access to a fake file, classification (C) labels sensitivity, and an access control vestibule is a physical control unrelated to file integrity.

questions.stem: A startup has almost no security budget but wants some way to detect tampering with its data and to catch intruders poking at fake bait. Per EK 5.6.B.1, which option correctly reflects the CED's statement about the cost of these controls?
options: Honeypots and hash-based integrity checks are inexpensive detective controls
Honeypots and hash checks are the most expensive detective controls available
Only a third-party DLP service can detect tampering, at a low cost
Hash checks are inexpensive, but honeypots require costly DLP licensing
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.B.1 names cost as a criterion for choosing detective controls and states that controls like honeypots and using hash values to check data integrity are inexpensive, while third-party DLP services provide strong detection at a higher cost. Options B, C, and D invert or misstate that cost relationship.

questions.stem: A large bank is deciding how closely to monitor two systems: a public marketing site and the core database holding customer account balances. Per EK 5.6.B.2, which criterion should drive monitoring the database more closely?
options: The age of the hardware the two systems run on
The number of employees who can log into each system
The sensitivity or criticality of the data, which makes it a likelier target
The programming language used to build each application
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.B.2 names sensitivity or criticality of data or applications as a criterion: more sensitive or critical data are more likely targets of an adversary and should be monitored more closely. Hardware age (A), user count (B), and programming language (D) are not the CED's stated criterion.

questions.stem: A hospital must decide what detection and monitoring it is required to put on its patient health records. Per EK 5.6.B.3, why does data classification factor into this decision?
options: Classified data is automatically encrypted, removing the need to monitor it
Data classified as healthcare often carries legal or regulatory monitoring requirements
Classification reduces the sensitivity of data so it needs less monitoring
Only financial data, never healthcare data, has detection requirements
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.B.3 names classification of data as a criterion and states that data classified as private, educational, healthcare, or financial often have legal or regulatory detection and monitoring requirements. Option A and C misdescribe classification, and Option D wrongly excludes healthcare data, which the CED explicitly lists.

questions.stem: A SOC manager complains that manually reading every log line is far too slow to catch attacks in time. Per EK 5.6.C.1, what does the CED say is needed for log analysis to operate at an effective speed, and what offers near-instant detection?
options: Log analysis must be done manually, and DLP offers near-instant detection
Log analysis needs no changes, and hashing offers near-instant detection
Log analysis must be slowed deliberately, and classification offers near-instant detection
Log analysis needs to be augmented with automation, and honeypots offer near-instant detection
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.C.1 states that to operate at an effective speed, log analysis needs to be augmented with some automation, and that honeypots offer near instantaneous detection capabilities. The other options contradict the CED on automation or attribute near-instant detection to the wrong control.

questions.stem: Per EK 5.6.C.2, which detection methods provide alerts as an attack is happening, allowing a prompt response? I. Honeypots. II. Real-time automated log analysis. III. Using cryptographic hashes to verify data integrity.
options: I and II only
I and III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.C.2 states that some DLP tools, honeypots, and real-time automated log analysis provide alerts as an attack is happening, enabling a prompt response, while retrospective log analysis and the use of cryptographic hashes to verify data integrity identify attacks after they have occurred. So I and II are real-time, but III (hashes) detects after the fact — the answer is I and II only.

questions.stem: An adversary copies a folder of confidential designs to a USB drive and walks out, but does not modify any of the original files. The team relied solely on cryptographic hash checks for detection. Per EK 5.6.C.3, what happened?
options: The hash check raised an alert as soon as the files were copied
The hash check corrected the breach by restoring the original files
A false negative occurred — hashes detect alteration, not viewing or theft
The hash values changed, revealing exactly which files were stolen
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.C.3 explains this false negative: cryptographic hash functions only detect whether data have been altered, so an adversary could view and steal data without altering it and the hash function would not detect it. The hash does not alert on copying (A), does not correct anything (B), and would not change if the originals were untouched (D).

questions.stem: A student hashes a download today, records the digest, then hashes the same unchanged file next week. Per EK 5.6.D.1, what property of cryptographic hash functions guarantees the two outputs match if the file is identical?
options: Hash functions are random, so each run produces a different digest
Hash functions are repeatable — the same input always yields the same output
Hash functions are reversible, so the file can be rebuilt from the digest
Hash functions are encrypted, so only the owner can read the digest
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.D.1 states that cryptographic hash functions can help identify changes in a file because they are repeatable: the same input always produces the same output for a given hash function. They are not random (A), they are not described as reversible (C), and the property at issue is repeatability, not encryption (D).

questions.stem: On a macOS machine using the zsh terminal, an analyst needs to generate the SHA-256 hash of a file named testfile. Per EK 5.6.D.2, which command does this?
options: Get-FileHash testfile -Algorithm SHA256
sha256sum testfile
Get-FileHash testfile -zsh 256
shasum -a 256 testfile
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.D.2 gives the zsh command on Apple computers as shasum -a 256 testfile. Get-FileHash (A) is the Windows PowerShell command, sha256sum (B) is the BASH command, and Option C is not a real command.

questions.stem: A defender recorded the SHA-256 hash of a critical file last month. Today the file produces a different hash than the recorded value. Per EK 5.6.D.3, what can be concluded?
options: The file was altered between when the first and second hashes were generated
The hash algorithm changed, but the file itself is unaltered
The file is corrupted only if a third hash also differs
Nothing can be concluded, since hash outputs vary on every run
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.D.3 states that a file can be hashed and its output recorded, then hashed again later and compared; if a file's hash changes, the file was altered between when the first and second hashes were generated. Because hashing is repeatable for a given function (EK 5.6.D.1), differing outputs for the same algorithm mean the file changed, ruling out B, C, and D.

questions.stem: While reviewing server logs, an analyst finds a request whose input field contains the text: ' OR 1=1 -- . Per EK 5.6.E.1, which application attack does this most clearly indicate?
options: A buffer overflow attack, shown by the long request length
A cross-site scripting attack, shown by an injected tag
A SQL injection attack, shown by a quote, a Boolean condition, and a comment
A directory traversal attack, shown by repeated path sequences
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.E.1 says SQL injection can be detected by reviewing logs for SQL control words and symbols such as a single or double quote, Boolean conditions like OR 1=1, and a double dash (--) comment. The input shown contains all three. XSS (B) uses <script> tags, buffer overflow (A) shows abnormally long fields, and directory traversal (D) shows ../ sequences.

questions.stem: An analyst spots an HTTP GET request whose path is /files/../../../../etc/passwd. Per EK 5.6.E.4, which attack does the repeated ../ sequence indicate?
options: An XSS attack, detected by the <script> tag in the request
A directory traversal attack, indicated by paths containing ../ sequences
A SQL injection attack, indicated by the WHERE and FROM keywords
A buffer overflow attack, indicated by an oversized cookie length
questions.rationale: EK 5.6.E.4 states that directory traversal attacks can be detected by reviewing logs, and that HTTP GET requests including paths with sequences of ../ are indicators of an adversary attempting a directory traversal — exactly the request shown. XSS (A) is flagged by <script> tags (EK 5.6.E.2), SQL injection (C) by SQL control words (EK 5.6.E.1), and buffer overflow (D) by abnormally long fields (EK 5.6.E.3).
